cards.text: Unscoped enumerators can be {{c1::implicitly converted}} to integral types, but integers {{c2::cannot}} be implicitly converted to enumerators.

cards.question: Does this code compile?
cards.code:
enum Color { red, green };
int x = red;
cards.answer: Yes. Unscoped enumerators implicitly convert to int. x = 0.
cards.explanation: Implicit conversion from enumerator to int is allowed.

cards.question: Does this code compile?
cards.code:
enum Color { red, green };
Color c = 0;
cards.answer: No, compile error. Cannot implicitly convert int to enum.
cards.explanation: Must use explicit cast: Color c = static_cast<Color>(0);

cards.text: To convert an int to an unscoped enum: Color c = {{c1::static_cast<Color>}}(0);

cards.front: Can you output an unscoped enumerator directly with std::cout?
cards.back:
Yes, but it prints the integer value, not the enumerator name.
Example: std::cout << Color::red; prints 0

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.code:
enum Color { red = 5, green, blue };
std::cout << green;
cards.explanation: Enumerators output as integers. green = red + 1 = 6.

cards.front: Why doesn't C++ allow implicit conversion from int to enum?
cards.back: To prevent accidentally assigning invalid values to an enum variable that don't correspond to any defined enumerator.

cards.concept: Enum to int conversions with unscoped enums
cards.explanation:
Conversion rules for unscoped enums:
  • Enum → int: Implicit (automatic)
  • Int → enum: Must use static_cast
  • Outputting enum prints integer value
  • This asymmetry prevents accidental invalid enum values
examples: Color c = 5;  // Error: no implicit int→enum

cards.question: What's problematic about this code?
cards.code:
enum Color { red, green, blue };
Color c = static_cast<Color>(100);
cards.answer: Compiles but creates invalid enum value (100 doesn't match any enumerator).
cards.explanation: Static cast doesn't validate the value. This can cause undefined behavior.

cards.front: How do you print an enumerator's name instead of its integer value?
cards.back: Write a custom function or use a switch statement to map values to strings. C++ doesn't have built-in enum-to-string conversion.

cards.text: Unscoped enumerators {{c1::can}} implicitly convert to int, but integers {{c2::cannot}} implicitly convert to enumerators.
To assign an integer to an unscoped enum variable: Color c = {{c1::static_cast<Color>}}(5);
Unscoped enumerators {{c1::can}} implicitly convert to int, but integers {{c2::cannot}} implicitly convert to enumerators.
